revision.text:
'''氏子狩'''（うじこがり）とは、強制的に自社の[[氏子]]に登録させること。特に滋賀県愛知郡小椋庄（神崎郡永源寺町、現：[[東近江市]]）の[[木地師]]に関する物が有名であり、本項ではそれについて解説する。
なお、ここでいう「狩り」とは、織田信長の「茶器名物狩り」のような「集める」という意味合いであって、捕まえる、殺すという意味ではない。
== 概要 == {{seealso|木地師}} 滋賀県愛知郡小椋庄は、木地師発祥の地とされていた。近世後期、神祗官の白川家擁する君ガ畑村の[[大皇器地祖神社|大皇太神（鏡寺）]]と、同じく神祗官の吉田家擁する蛭谷村の[[筒井神社|筒井八幡（帰雲庵）]]は、木地師の祖とされる[[惟喬親王]]の霊社を祭り、これを木地師の氏神と喧伝した。
当時、木地師は全国各地に散っており、時に朝廷や幕府の許可を受けて、生業を続けていた。もし、これら木地師達を氏子として自社の影響下に置くことができれば、大きな収入を得ることに繋がる。
そこで白川・吉田両家は、自らを正当な惟喬親王の霊社としつつ、各地に散った木地師達を探し出し、自社の氏子に強制的に登録していった。両社は時に偽造文書も発行し、自社の配下に収めるために争った。
結果として木地師はどちらかの神社に登録されることとなり、金を納めて一定の義務を背負った。また、両社はその権威を振りかざして木地屋の営業認可まで行った。実際に惟喬親王が祖だったかは不明であるが、一連の出来事によって木地師達は惟喬親王が業祖であることを信じていた。
この氏子狩りの大きさは、文化13年（1816年）の筒井八幡立替の際には、36ヶ国約3600名余りの木地師が氏子として費用を献じたことからもわかる。
== 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』「氏子狩」永島福太郎 [[吉川弘文館]]1983年 * 『日本歴史大辞典1』「氏子狩」平山敏治郎[[河出書房新社]] 1985年
{{DEFAULTSORT:うしこかり}} [[Category:神社]]

'''氏子狩'''（うじこがり）とは、強制的に自社の[[氏子]]に登録させること。特に[[滋賀県]][[愛知郡 (滋賀県)|愛知郡]]小椋庄（[[神崎郡 (滋賀県)|神崎郡]][[永源寺町]]、現：[[東近江市]]）の[[木地師]]に関する物が有名であり、本項ではそれについて解説する。
なお、ここでいう「狩り」とは、[[織田信長]]の「茶器名物狩り」のような「集める」という意味合いであって、捕まえる、殺すという意味ではない。
== 概要 == {{seealso|木地師}} 滋賀県愛知郡小椋庄は、木地師発祥の地とされていた。近世後期、神祗官の白川家擁する君ガ畑村の[[大皇器地祖神社|大皇太神（鏡寺）]]と、同じく神祗官の吉田家擁する蛭谷村の[[筒井神社|筒井八幡（帰雲庵）]]は、木地師の祖とされる[[惟喬親王]]の霊社を祀り、これを木地師の氏神と喧伝した。
当時、木地師は全国各地に散っており、時に[[朝廷]]や[[幕府]]の許可を受けて、生業を続けていた。もし、これら木地師達を氏子として自社の影響下に置くことができれば、大きな収入を得ることに繋がる。
そこで白川・吉田両家は、自らを正当な惟喬親王の霊社としつつ、各地に散った木地師達を探し出し、自社の氏子に強制的に登録していった。両社は時に偽造文書も発行し、自社の配下に収めるために争った。
結果として木地師はどちらかの神社に登録されることとなり、金を納めて一定の義務を背負った。また、両社はその権威を振りかざして木地屋の営業認可まで行なった。実際に惟喬親王が祖だったかは不明であるが、一連の出来事によって木地師達は惟喬親王が業祖であることを信じていた。
この氏子狩りの大きさは、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]13年（[[1816年]]）の筒井八幡立替の際には、36ヶ国約3600名余りの木地師が氏子として費用を献じたことからもわかる。
== 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』「氏子狩」永島福太郎、[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年 * 『日本歴史大辞典1』「氏子狩」平山敏治郎、[[河出書房新社]]、1985年
{{DEFAULTSORT:うしこかり}} [[Category:神社]]

なお、ここでいう「狩り」とは、[[織田信長]]の「茶器名物狩り」のような「集める」という意味合いであって、捕まえる、殺すという意味ではない。

当時、木地師は全国各地に散っており、時に[[朝廷]]や[[幕府]]の許可を受けて、生業を続けていた。もし、これら木地師達を氏子として自社の影響下に置くことができれば、大きな収入を得ることに繋がる。
そこで白川・吉田両家は、自らを正当な惟喬親王の霊社としつつ、各地に散った木地師達を探し出し、自社の氏子に強制的に登録していった。両社は時に偽造文書も発行し、自社の配下に収めるために争った。
結果として木地師はどちらかの神社に登録されることとなり、金を納めて一定の義務を背負った。また、両社はその権威を振りかざして木地屋の営業認可まで行なった。実際に惟喬親王が祖だったかは不明であるが、一連の出来事によって木地師達は惟喬親王が業祖であることを信じていた。
この氏子狩りの大きさは、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]13年（[[1816年]]）の筒井八幡立替の際には、36ヶ国約3600名余りの木地師が氏子として費用を献じたことからもわかる。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』「氏子狩」永島福太郎、[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年 * 『日本歴史大辞典1』「氏子狩」平山敏治郎、[[河出書房新社]]、1985年

'''氏子狩'''（うじこがり、'''氏子駈'''とも）とは、強制的に自社の[[氏子]]に登録させること。特に[[滋賀県]][[愛知郡 (滋賀県)|愛知郡]][[小椋庄]]（現：[[東近江市]]<ref>後の[[東小椋村]]; 地名としては[[西小椋村]]、[[豊椋村]]にも残る; [http://ochiel.sakura.ne.jp/life/folk/index.html], [http://www.slownet.ne.jp/sns/area/travel/reading/gen_readig/201009011354-9781523.html]</ref>、「[[小椋谷]]」）の[[木地師]]に関する物が有名であり、本項ではそれについて解説する。
なお、ここでいう「狩り」とは、[[織田信長]]の「茶器名物狩り」のような「集める」という意味合いであって、捕まえる、殺すという意味ではない。
== 概要 == {{seealso|木地師}} 滋賀県愛知郡小椋庄は、木地師発祥の地とされていた。近世後期、[[神祗官]]の[[白川伯王家|白川家]]擁する[[君ガ畑村]]<ref>滋賀県[[東近江市]][[君ヶ畑町]]。[[御池川]]流域</ref>の[[大皇器地祖神社|大皇太神（鏡寺）]]と、同じく神祗官の[[吉田家]]擁する[[蛭谷村]]の[[筒井神社|筒井八幡（帰雲庵）]]<ref>東近江市（[[永源寺町]]）[[蛭谷]]。御池川流域; [http://www.norichan.jp/jinja/shigoto2/tsutsui.htm]</ref>は、木地師の祖とされる[[惟喬親王]]の霊社を祀り、これを木地師の氏神と喧伝した。
当時、木地師は全国各地に散っており、時に[[朝廷]]や[[幕府]]の許可を受けて、生業を続けていた。もし、これら木地師達を氏子として自社の影響下に置くことができれば、大きな収入を得ることに繋がる。
そこで白川・吉田両家は、自らを正当な惟喬親王の霊社としつつ、各地に散った木地師達を探し出し、自社の氏子に強制的に登録していった。両社は時に偽造文書も発行し、自社の配下に収めるために争った。
結果として木地師はどちらかの神社に登録されることとなり、金を納めて一定の義務を背負った。また、両社はその権威を振りかざして木地屋の営業認可まで行なった。実際に惟喬親王が祖だったかは不明であるが、一連の出来事によって木地師達は惟喬親王が業祖であることを信じていた。
この氏子狩りの大きさは、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]13年（[[1816年]]）の筒井八幡立替の際には、36ヶ国約3600名余りの木地師が氏子として費用を献じたことからもわかる。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』「氏子狩」永島福太郎、[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年 * 『日本歴史大辞典1』「氏子狩」平山敏治郎、[[河出書房新社]]、1985年
{{DEFAULTSORT:うしこかり}} [[Category:日本の歴史]] [[Category:神社]] [[Category:日本美術史]] [[Category:滋賀県の歴史]] [[Category:東近江市の歴史]] [[Category:愛知郡 (滋賀県)]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=滋賀県}}

revision.text:
'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[Kijishi]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōmikami Shrine|ja|大皇器地祖神社}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社}}, 1985.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Shiga Prefecture}} [[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Shrines]] [[Category:Japanese art history]] [[Category:History of Higashiōmi]] [[ja:氏子狩]]

The most well-known example is associated with the [[Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). This article focuses on that case.

'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōmikami Shrine|ja|大皇器地祖神社}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町}}). '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市}}). This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

revision.text:
'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市|12=draft}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師|12=draft}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官|12=draft}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=draft}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=draft}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=draft}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=draft}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=draft}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化|12=draft}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|12=draft}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社|12=draft}}, 1985.

'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市|12=draft}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師|12=draft}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官|12=draft}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=draft}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=draft}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=draft}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=draft}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=draft}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=draft}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|12=draft}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|12=draft}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社|12=draft}}, 1985.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Shiga Prefecture}}
{{citation needed|Ujikogari}}

revision.text:
'''氏子狩'''（うじこがり、'''氏子駈'''とも）とは、強制的に自社の[[氏子]]に登録させること。特に[[滋賀県]][[愛知郡 (滋賀県)|愛知郡]]小椋庄（現：[[東近江市]]）の[[木地師]]に関する物が有名であり、本項ではそれについて解説する。
なお、ここでいう「狩り」とは、[[織田信長]]の「茶器名物狩り」のような「集める」という意味合いであって、捕まえる、殺すという意味ではない。
== 概要 == {{seealso|木地師}} 滋賀県愛知郡小椋庄は、木地師発祥の地とされていた。近世後期、[[神祗官]]の[[白川伯王家|白川家]]擁する[[君ガ畑村]]（東近江市君ヶ畑町）の[[大皇器地祖神社|大皇太神（鏡寺）]]と、同じく神祗官の[[吉田家]]擁する蛭谷村の[[筒井神社|筒井八幡（帰雲庵）]]（東近江市蛭谷町）は、木地師の祖とされる[[惟喬親王]]の霊社を祀り、これを木地師の氏神と喧伝した。
当時、木地師は全国各地に散っており、時に[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]や[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の許可を受けて、生業を続けていた。もし、これら木地師達を氏子として自社の影響下に置くことができれば、大きな収入を得ることに繋がる。
そこで白川・吉田両家は、自らを正当な惟喬親王の霊社としつつ、各地に散った木地師達を探し出し、自社の氏子に強制的に登録していった。両社は時に偽造文書も発行し、自社の配下に収めるために争った。
結果として木地師はどちらかの神社に登録されることとなり、金を納めて一定の義務を背負った。また、両社はその権威を振りかざして木地屋の営業認可まで行なった。実際に惟喬親王が祖だったかは不明であるが、一連の出来事によって木地師達は惟喬親王が業祖であることを信じていた。
この氏子狩りの大きさは、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]13年（[[1816年]]）の筒井八幡立替の際には、36ヶ国約3600名余りの木地師が氏子として費用を献じたことからもわかる。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』「氏子狩」永島福太郎、[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年。 * 『日本歴史大辞典1』「氏子狩」平山敏治郎、[[河出書房新社]]、1985年。
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=滋賀県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:うしこかり}} [[Category:日本の歴史]] [[Category:神社]] [[Category:日本美術史]] [[Category:東近江市の歴史]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiōmi|qq=}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kijishi|qq=}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官|1=Department of Divinities|2=ja|3=神祇官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingikan|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|qq=}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|qq=}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|qq=}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|qq=}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|qq=}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|qq=}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|qq=}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawade Shobō Shinsha|qq=}}, 1985.

[[ja:氏子狩]] [[sn:Ujikogari]]

'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|ja|愛知郡 (滋賀県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiōmi|qq=}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijishi|ja|木地師|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kijishi|qq=}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祗官|1=Department of Divinities|2=ja|3=神祇官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingikan|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|qq=}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|qq=}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|qq=}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|qq=}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|qq=}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|qq=}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化 (元号)|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|qq=}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawade Shobō Shinsha|qq=}}, 1985.

Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|qq=}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|qq=}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|qq=}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|qq=}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

revision.text:
'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|qq=}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|qq=}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|qq=}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11547792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.

The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.

== Overview ==
{{seealso|Kijishi}}
Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.

At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.

Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.

As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.

The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11547792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, [[:en:Kawade Shobō Shinsha|Kawade Shobō Shinsha]], 1985.

== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11547792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of [[:en:Kijiya|Kijishi]]. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11547792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.

As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏子狩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11547792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ([[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]]) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the [[:en:Kijiya|Kijiya]] (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in [[:en:Echi District, Shiga|Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture]] (modern-day [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi]]). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.

Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.

The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in [[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, [[:en:Kawade Shobō Shinsha|Kawade Shobō Shinsha]], 1985.

The most well-known example is associated with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Kijiya}} (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Echi District, Shiga|en|Echi District, Shiga|WD=Q1131097|lt=Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.

The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Kijishi}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.

The most well-known example is associated with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Kijiya|1=Kijiya}} (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Echi District, Shiga|en|Echi District, Shiga|WD=Q1131097|lt=Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|1=Echi District, Shiga}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi|1=Higashiōmi}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.
== Overview == {{seealso|Kijishi}} Ogura Shō in Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture, is considered the birthplace of {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Kijishi|1=Kijiya}}. In the late Edo period, the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川伯王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|qq=}} of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} oversaw the {{ill|Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|ja|大皇器地祖神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkimi kijiso-jinja|1=Ōkimi Kijiso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438052}} (also known as Daitakami or Kyōji) in {{ill|Kimi no Hatamura|ja|君ガ畑村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市君ヶ畑町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimi no Hatamachi, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court|1=Royal court}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
The scale of the ujikogari is evident from the reconstruction of Tsutsui Hachiman Shrine in {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}} 13 (1816), which was funded by approximately 3,600 Kijishi from 36 provinces who were registered as ujiko.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}, "Ujikogari" by Fukutaro Nagashima, {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983. * {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Vol. 1, "Ujikogari" by Toshijiro Hirayama, {{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|WD=Q11141263|lt=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|1=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}, 1985.

revision.text:
'''Ujikogari''' (氏子狩, also known as '''Ujikokake''' [氏子駈]) refers to the forced registration of individuals as parishioners ({{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|WD=Q125562175|lt=Ujiko|1=Ujiko}}) of a shrine. The most well-known example is associated with the {{ill|Kijiya|en|Kijiya|WD=Q11518247|lt=Kijiya|1=Kijiya}} (woodturners) of [[Ogura Shō]] in {{ill|Echi District, Shiga|en|Echi District, Shiga|WD=Q1131097|lt=Aichi District, Shiga Prefecture|1=Echi District, Shiga}} (modern-day {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi|1=Higashiōmi}}). <ref>https://bokujinshi.info/eq&a.htm</ref>This article focuses on that case.
The term "kagari" (狩り) here implies "gathering," as in {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s "collection of famous tea utensils" (茶器名物狩り), rather than its meanings of capturing or killing.

Similarly, the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}}, also of the Jingikan, oversaw {{ill|Tsutsui Shrine|ja|筒井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsui Shrine|1=Tsutsui Shrine (Higashiomi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119929648}} (also known as Tsutsui Hachiman or Kaeriumian) in {{ill|Hirugai Village|ja|蛭谷村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day {{ill|Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja|東近江市蛭谷町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirugai Town, Higashiōmi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Both shrines enshrined the spirit of {{ill|Prince Koretaka|ja|惟喬親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Koretaka|1=Koretaka-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492950}}, who was believed to be the ancestral deity of the Kijishi, and proclaimed this widely.
At the time, Kijishi were dispersed across the country, sometimes practicing their trade with permission from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court|1=Royal court}} or the {{ill|Edo shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Bringing these scattered Kijishi under the influence of their respective shrines would yield significant income for the shrines.
Thus, the Shirakawa and Yoshida families sought out these Kijishi, claiming their shrines to be the legitimate spiritual sites of Prince Koretaka. They forcibly registered Kijishi as ujiko and sometimes issued forged documents to assert their authority. This competition for control often led to disputes between the two shrines.
As a result, the Kijishi were registered with either shrine, paid monetary contributions, and assumed certain obligations. The shrines also wielded their authority to grant or deny business licenses to the Kijishi. While it remains unclear whether Prince Koretaka was truly their ancestor, the Kijishi came to believe he was their occupational founder due to these events.
